strap: A daily dispatch from the world you are entering

lead.title: Security becomes a department of the house, with its own pay scales, headcount and shortages
lead.standfirst: The 2026 protective benchmarks put a detail agent at USD 128,000 median base, and a family of four under continuous coverage at an annual payroll of USD 1.8m to 2.4m.
lead.body:
Close protection is no longer a service bought in for big journeys, it has become a permanent function of the private house. The 2026 compensation benchmarks for the US market place the executive protection agent at USD 128,000 median base, the detail leader at USD 185,000, and the director of security at USD 230,000. Total compensation adds a multiple of 1.20 to 1.45 times base for the agent, and 1.30 to 1.65 times for the director, including bonus, deferred compensation and programme management incentives.

The question families ask has changed in kind. It is no longer what an agent costs, but how many agents are required, which is a headcount calculation closer to running a hotel department than to hiring a bodyguard. Continuous coverage of a single principal in a static residence needs a minimum of 5 to 6 agents, and 6 to 8 to absorb training, holidays and unplanned absence. A principal travelling continuously needs 8 to 12 agents across the residential detail, the travelling sub-team and advance work. A family of four under continuous coverage needs 12 to 16 agents, an annual payroll of USD 1.8m to 2.4m in baseline US markets, rising above USD 3m in New York or Aspen.

Geography adds its own multipliers. Aspen leads at 1.50 to 1.55 times base with the thinnest qualified pool of any market, ahead of New York, the Hamptons, San Francisco and Geneva at 1.40, then Greenwich, Palm Beach and Monaco at 1.35. London sits at 1.20 and Dubai at 1.10 to 1.15, a figure that misleads: with no income tax, a nominal Dubai base of USD 141,000 comfortably exceeds a Geneva base of USD 179,000 after deductions.

The market tightened sharply after late 2024. Demand for certified protective personnel rose 75 per cent year on year, ad hoc travel security by 300 per cent, and median per-executive security spend moved from USD 69,000 to USD 94,000 a year. The ASIS executive protection standard published in September 2025 explicitly recommends that the director of security should not personally stand a close-protection post, the two disciplines being distinct. Mature programmes now separate the functions cleanly.

The point of contact with the rest of the house is constant. The director of security reports to the chief of staff in 45 per cent of programmes, to a family office director in 30 per cent, and directly to the principal in 25 per cent. Vendor access control, background vetting of household staff and training the team on confidentiality protocols all sit inside that daily coordination. Non-disclosure agreements are standard before hire, and firms prefer candidates with no social media presence.
lead.why: Security and household service now share the same corridors, the same rosters and often the same line manager. Reading a protective headcount, a rotation pattern and a confidentiality clause is part of the estate manager's job.
lead.src: Talent Gurus, Executive Protection Salary Guide 2026

articles.title: The wealthy family stops choosing a country and starts assembling a portfolio of jurisdictions
articles.body:
The 2026 Henley & Partners report on private wealth migration abandons the logic of inflow and outflow league tables for a framework that scores jurisdictions across twelve dimensions and thirty-eight weighted indicators. Its central finding is that the most mobile families no longer pick a destination country. They build what the report calls a sovereign portfolio, combining residence rights, citizenships, investment structures and education options spread across several jurisdictions.

The demand figures give the measure of it. In the first five months of 2026 the firm received applications from 86 nationalities across 47 investment migration programmes. More than 28 per cent of applicants already live outside their country of nationality. The stated objective is not departure but distribution, so that no single government holds the whole of a family's life and capital.

The competitiveness ranking puts Singapore first at 79.5 out of 100, ahead of New Zealand at 75.8, then the Cayman Islands, Cyprus, the Netherlands, Portugal, Italy and Bermuda. The United Arab Emirates score 85.3, among the highest in the framework, despite regional tensions. At the other end, Germany at 69.7, Norway at 69.0, the United Kingdom at 68.3 and France at 65.7 are classed as competitive jurisdictions under pressure.

Application flows confirm those positions. Applications from UK addresses rose 15 per cent between 2024 and 2025, and British citizens now account for almost half of all applications originating in the UK, against 8 per cent in 2018. Applications from US nationals doubled in 2025 and remain elevated in 2026, with only 7 per cent coming from Americans living abroad. The firm recorded a 16 per cent rise in enquiries from German nationals between Q4 2025 and Q1 2026, and France moved from its top 40 source nationalities in 2024 to its top 15 in 2026.

The Gulf case deserves precise reading. Enquiries from UAE-based individuals rose 41 per cent between Q4 2025 and Q1 2026, and applications for alternative residence or citizenship by 29 per cent. The report reads this as diversification and contingency planning, driven by expatriate entrepreneurs and internationally mobile families using the country as a base, rather than as an exodus.
articles.why: A family spread across four jurisdictions does not want the same service four times over. It wants staff who can track tax presence calendars, distinct employment law regimes and houses that open and close in rotation.
articles.src: Henley & Partners, Private Wealth Migration Report 2026

articles.title: The global ultra wealthy class now numbers 556,850 people
articles.body:
Altrata's World Ultra Wealth Report 2026 puts the global ultra high net worth population, defined from USD 30m in net worth, at 556,850 individuals, an all-time high. Growth reached 14.4 per cent across 2025, the strongest since 2017, and marks a second consecutive year of double-digit expansion. Over two years the population has grown by almost 30 per cent, adding more than 120,000 people.

Combined wealth in this group stands at USD 63.8 trillion, more than double the annual GDP of the United States. Concentration is the striking feature: the ultra wealthy make up 1.1 per cent of the global millionaire population yet command 32 per cent of its cumulative wealth. North America, Asia and Europe are home to more than 90 per cent of the class.

The regional dynamic is shifting. Asia recorded 15.8 per cent growth in its ultra wealthy population in 2025 and is forecast to post the strongest expansion of the three major regions to 2030. Altrata projects a global population of 746,570 individuals by then, with combined wealth of USD 85 trillion, a third larger than the 2025 level.

What these numbers describe, seen from private service, is not a statistical abstraction. Each crossing of the USD 30m threshold corresponds, statistically, to a household that acquires one or more additional residences, structures a family office, or delegates the running of its domestic life to someone else. The pool of positions follows that curve with a lag of a few years.
articles.why: The number of houses that need running tracks the number of fortunes crossing the thirty million line. It is the most reliable base for sizing the private service job market.

articles.title: Charter booking lead times fall from 118 days to 83
articles.body:
Booking data through the end of May describes a charter market where the decision is being taken much closer to departure. Average booking lead time has fallen from 118 days in 2025 to 83 days in 2026, a reduction of almost 30 per cent year on year. It is the clearest shift in booking behaviour observed this season.

The compression in timing is striking. Nearly one third of forward June 2026 bookings were for charters beginning in June itself, and more than 60 per cent for departures before the end of July. Of 1,162 forward bookings recorded in June, 939, or 80.8 per cent, were for charters taking place between June and August. Brokers read this less as a lasting change in habit than as a response to geopolitical uncertainty, with clients reluctant to commit far ahead.

The geography, by contrast, has not moved. The Western Mediterranean, Eastern Mediterranean and Adriatic account for almost 80 per cent of forward June bookings, the Western Mediterranean alone representing 546 bookings, close to half the month's activity. Athens remains the leading embarkation point with 161 bookings, ahead of Nice with 53 and Monaco with 46.

Charter length is edging up. Median duration moved from seven days in June 2025 to eight days in June 2026, and the eight to ten day band has become the largest booking category with 499 bookings, or 42.9 per cent of activity. The traditional week has lost first place. For crew, a preparation window shortened by 35 days combined with longer voyages mechanically changes the rhythm of provisioning, relief hiring and itinerary planning.
articles.why: An order book that fills three weeks before departure forces crew and shore-side concierges to hold supply chains and itineraries ready without visibility. That is a skill of anticipation, not of reaction.

articles.title: The collector car market splits in two, a top that holds and a middle that gives way
articles.body:
The collector car market in 2026 shows an unusual gap between its summit and its body. Hagerty's Blue Chip Index, tracking the most sought-after pieces, sits at 75.54, while the Hagerty Hundred, a weighted average of condition number 2 values across the hundred most insured vehicles, has slipped to 48.32. Nearly twenty-seven points separate the two measures.

The Hagerty Hundred peaked in May 2022 above USD 50,000 in average value. It now stands at USD 43,408 and, adjusted for inflation, is at an all-time low. Hagerty's broader Market Index has gained ground for three consecutive months to 172.5, but the Market Rating has trended lower for six months and the auction median sale price has set a fresh low in real terms.

The practical reading of that gap is simple. Rare, documented pieces continue to clear seven figures, while good driver-quality classics have become markedly easier to buy. The market is not falling, it is polarising, and original documentation and service history are what decide which side of the line a car falls on.

The Monterey sales in August draw the deepest pool of high-end buyers in the world. They will show whether the strength at the top observed in the first half carries into the autumn. For a house holding a collection garage, the interval is precisely when insured value and market value can diverge significantly by segment.
articles.why: A collection garage is an inventory and insurance line managed through files, not memories. A polarised market makes maintenance history decisive for value.

articles.title: In Meursault, ten rooms built around the winemaker's craft
articles.body:
Maison Le Chevreuil opens this July in Meursault, one of the most celebrated villages of the Côte de Beaune, with ten rooms designed around the art of winemaking. The format is that of the small Burgundian village house rather than the destination hotel, in a commune whose entire reputation rests on its whites.

The choice of location says something about the moment. Meursault is not a resort, it is a working wine village where access to the domaines runs through relationships rather than reservations. A ten-room house that settles there is addressing guests who have come to understand an appellation, not to be seen.

For a private concierge, this is precisely the kind of address that gains value in a contacts book. Requests for Burgundy tastings run into a severely constrained supply of domaines, where visits cannot be bought and an introduction counts for more than a budget. A well-run local base within walking distance of the cellars is a more useful lever than a palace hotel a hundred kilometres away.
articles.why: Cultural fluency in wine is judged on the ability to secure a visit where visits are not sold. Knowing the village addresses of Burgundy is worth more than a directory of grand hotels.

briefs.t: Business aviation advances, except in the Middle East
briefs.b: Global business jet activity rose 3.9 per cent across the first half of 2026, and 2.7 per cent in June alone. North America gained 4.6 per cent and Africa 5.2 per cent, while Europe slipped 0.5 per cent. The rest of the world fell 2.6 per cent, driven by a 15 per cent contraction in the Middle East.

briefs.t: The rarest post in private security
briefs.b: The technical surveillance counter-measures specialist, responsible for detecting listening devices, cameras and trackers in residences and private aircraft, carries a scarcity rating of 10 out of 10. The US pool holds 12 to 40 candidates, every one trained in a government counter-intelligence school, with no civilian pipeline. Time to fill runs to 24 weeks for a median salary of USD 140,000.

briefs.t: Delhi leads ultra wealthy growth to 2030
briefs.b: Altrata forecasts Delhi as the fastest-growing city by ultra wealthy population to 2030, followed by Stockholm and Wuhan. The projection carries the global population to 746,570 individuals holding USD 85 trillion. The new maps of private service recruitment are being drawn along these urban trajectories.

briefs.t: Greece and Italy capture the redirected demand
briefs.b: Greece, scored at 70.5 in the Henley 2026 framework, is among the clearest beneficiaries of Spain's golden visa closure and Portugal's withdrawal of its real estate route. Italy, at 72.3, is drawing on its flat-tax regime for new residents and its inheritance framework, with Milan establishing itself as a family office centre. When a government closes a pathway, demand does not disappear, it relocates.
